input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin system leverages Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies with SysML for system modeling, MathWorks Simulink for simulation, and Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, and MontiArc for component and connector architecture specification. Behavior modeling is achieved through MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, integrated with general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) and other simulation tools for detailed dynamic analysis.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Data from physical assets is streamed via MQTT in real-time, capturing live measurements to ensure continuous synchronization between the physical and digital components with minimal latency.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Shadow Reference Model, defined as a UML class diagram, structures components including DataTrace (for contextual data collection), Source (data origin), and Asset (physical entities), with defined relationships for aggregation and abstraction of data traces.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems uses Asset Administration Shells (AAS) as an interoperability standard, MQTT for real-time data exchange, and interfaces with the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller for industrial automation processes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include structural component & connector descriptions via ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs) detailing system components, interfaces, and interconnections; behavioral models implemented using MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7 state machines; and detailed simulation models integrated with MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data, including input current and output angle, are streamed from physical components to the digital shadow using MQTT protocol for real-time data transmission.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process utilizes methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for system complexity management. Architecture description languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs model components, interfaces, and interconnections, with behavior described via MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines. Detailed simulation is achieved using MathWorks Simulink, specialized simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages like C++ or Java.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Digital shadows enable continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction using white-box and black-box models to enhance robot motion accuracy. Decision support is provided through dashboard visualizations for user interaction.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with industry standards including SysML for systems engineering modeling, AADL for architecture description, ACME for component-based architecture, and MontiArc for modular architecture design. Interoperability is addressed through the integration of heterogeneous modeling notations and tools as per MBSE practices.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The document explicitly describes the physical system being studied, which is the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot. The extraction incorrectly states 'Not in Document' despite the text detailing the robot's components, controller, and use case. Evidence: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor'.

The paper defines its subject as a 'Digital Shadow', which it explicitly states is a 'passive entity' that 'neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system', distinguishing it from a Digital Twin that provides active services. Since the document focuses on a passive data model rather than active services (like control or optimization) provided by a Digital Twin, stating the characteristic is 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction accurately lists the tools and methodologies described in Section II.A. It correctly identifies Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE), SysML, MathWorks Simulink, specific Architecture Description Languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc), and general-purpose languages (C++, Java) as tools used for system modeling and simulation.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies that data is 'streamed via MQTT' and involves 'live data' and 'real-time' aspects as described in the methodology section. However, the text specifies a 'one-way data flow' (shadow), whereas the extraction uses the term 'synchronization', which typically implies bidirectional adjustment. Additionally, 'minimal latency' is implied by real-time but not explicitly stated as a requirement.

The document focuses on a single digital shadow for the 'first axis of the MABI robot' and does not mention multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between multiple twins. No relevant evidence exists.

The description accurately reflects the Digital Shadow Reference Model presented in Section II.B. It correctly identifies the model as a 'UML class diagram' and lists the core components 'DataTrace', 'Source', and 'Asset', as well as the concept of 'aggregation and abstraction' of data traces.

The extraction correctly identifies the interoperability standard (Asset Administration Shells or AAS), the data exchange protocol (MQTT), and the specific external hardware controller (Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl) mentioned in the text ('The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl').

To achieve the necessary low gear ratio, a gear transmission is first used... followed by a cycloidal gear'.

The source documents describe the sensing infrastructure in the Use Case section, specifying that 'There are two position encoders per drive' (motor encoder and secondary encoder) and listing available sensor data like 'input current' and 'output angle'. These are the input current and the output angle.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. A review of the Source Documents reveals that while the text discusses improving 'robot motion accuracy' and using models for 'accurate friction modeling', it does not provide specific details regarding model fidelity requirements (e.g., specific error margins), formal validation and verification methods, uncertainty quantification, or quality assurance processes as defined in the characteristic description. The documents focus on the *application* of models to achieve accuracy rather than defining the fidelity and validity specifications themselves. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is the correct assessment.

It does not contain any information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection, consent management, or data anonymization. The description 'Not in Document' is accurate. The source text details the technical implementation of digital shadows, friction models, and data integration for an industrial robot.
